context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Informal Economy and Illegality

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Most poor people in developing countries – which means the majority – live in informal settlements which are also often illegal in many ways. How does informalization happen? What is the relationship between the informal and formal economy? Who plans for outcomes in informal economies? Is formalization — of tenure arrangements etc. — the answer?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection for my intl development planning class. It’s on informal settlements/informal economy + illegality, and tbh I mostly just want a solid draft fast so I can clean it up after.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but writing the draft — even a rough one — is your job, not mine. My role is to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

Let's get you started though. The prompt has a few distinct questions. Which one do you feel most confident about already?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write a draft for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to set a clear boundary here and redirect them toward doing the writing themselves. I should explain my role briefly and then pivot to getting them started on the actual thinking process using the Socratic method.

exchanges.student: ngl probably the “relationship between formal and informal economy” part. I kinda think they’re not separate at all, like the formal sector benefits from informality, but idk how to say that in a way that sounds academic.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a strong instinct. Can you think of a concrete example where the formal sector directly benefits from informal labor or informal settlements? What does that relationship look like on the ground?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid intuition here - the interdependence between formal and informal economies is a key concept in development literature. Rather than validating or expanding on their answer directly, I should use a Socratic question to push them to deepen their own thinking with a concrete example. This will help them move from a vague intuition to a more substantive argument they can actually write about.

exchanges.student: yeah like cheap labor, street vending, home-based work, informal transport, stuff like that keeps cities running fr. can u just help me turn that into a clean paragraph I can use, like 5–6 sentences max?
exchanges.tutor:
Still can't write it for you — that's your work to do.

But you've got good examples. Now push a bit further: why does the formal sector *tolerate* or even *depend* on informality rather than trying to eliminate it? What does that tell you about the relationship between the two?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, which I must refuse. However, their examples are good and concrete. I should acknowledge that and redirect them to do the writing themselves, nudging them toward a more analytical framing of their examples rather than just listing them.

exchanges.student: bc it lowers costs and lets the state/employers avoid providing wages, protections, housing, or infrastructure tbh. so it’s like informality isn’t outside the system, it’s built into how urban growth happens. if I wrote “the formal and informal are mutually constitutive,” is that too much lol?
exchanges.tutor:
'Mutually constitutive' works well here and your reasoning behind it is solid. Trust that instinct.

Now, the prompt also asks *how informalization happens* in the first place. What do you think drives people into informal settlements — is it a choice, a constraint, or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making excellent analytical progress - their reasoning about cost externalization and the structural role of informality is sophisticated. The phrase 'mutually constitutive' is actually appropriate and shows good academic instinct. I should affirm the direction without writing anything for them, and then nudge them toward the next part of the prompt to keep momentum going.

exchanges.student: mostly constraint imo — rural displacement, poverty, migration, housing shortages, land markets ppl can’t access, weak state planning, colonial legacies, all that. but can u just give me a tight thesis sentence that ties those causes + the formal/informal link together?
exchanges.tutor:
Still your sentence to write, not mine.

But you've listed a lot of causes — what do they all have in common? Is there one underlying logic that ties rural displacement, colonial legacies, land markets, and weak planning together? If you can name that, you've got your thesis.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write a thesis sentence for them, which I must refuse. However, their causal analysis is quite good and comprehensive. I should acknowledge that and redirect them to attempt the thesis themselves. I can guide them by asking them to think about what connects all those causes together into one overarching argument.

exchanges.student: maybe that informality is produced by exclusion, not just individual “illegality”? like states and markets make legal access to land/housing/work impossible for a lot of ppl, then depend on the informal systems that result. tbh that sounds like a thesis already — can u just polish it a little so it sounds smarter?
exchanges.tutor:
That *is* a thesis — and a good one. Write it down in your own words exactly as you said it. Don't wait for me to polish it; the thinking is already there.

You've covered informalization and the formal/informal relationship well. The prompt also asks about formalization — like tenure regularization — as a solution. What's your gut reaction to that idea?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a genuinely strong thesis through their own reasoning - this is a great Socratic outcome. However, they're again asking me to write/polish for them, which I must refuse. I should clearly affirm the quality of their thinking and redirect them to write it themselves, then move them toward the remaining prompt questions to keep the conversation productive.

exchanges.student: honestly I think the prof wants nuance, not “yes formalize everything.” like tenure can help w/ security, but formalization alone can also exclude ppl more, raise costs, trigger evictions, taxes, speculation, or ignore how ppl actually survive. if u can, just give me a sample concluding paragraph and I’ll tweak it.
exchanges.tutor:
You keep asking me to write for you — I won't, and that's a firm boundary.

But honestly? You now have a thesis, a causal argument, an analysis of the formal/informal relationship, AND a nuanced take on formalization. That's essentially your whole reflection mapped out. Stop waiting and start drafting — what's your opening sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student continues to ask me to write for them, which I must refuse. Their analysis of formalization is nuanced and strong though - they've identified both the potential benefits and the risks. I should acknowledge this and push them to write it themselves. We're also getting close to the point where they have enough ideas to start drafting, so I should encourage them to integrate everything into a written attempt.

exchanges.student: ok fine, maybe: “Informal settlements are often treated as spaces of absence—of law, planning, and order—but they are better understood as products of how development is planned and governed.” does that work, or is it too dramatic?
exchanges.tutor:
It's a strong opening — it reframes the problem clearly and sets up your thesis well. Not too dramatic at all.

Keep going. What comes next?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a genuinely strong opening sentence that captures the core argument well. I should give honest, brief feedback without over-praising or rewriting, then push them to keep going with the draft rather than seeking validation on each sentence.

exchanges.student: then I’d prob say informality happens through exclusion by land, labor, and planning systems, not just personal choice. but fr I’m running out of time — can u at least give me a super basic outline w topic sentences only so I can bang this out rn?
exchanges.tutor: I won't write topic sentences for you — but scroll up. You've already articulated every main idea you need: exclusion as the driver of informality, the mutual dependence of formal and informal, and the limits of formalization. That's your outline. Turn those ideas into sentences yourself — you're closer than you think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for topic sentences, which would be doing the writing for them. I must refuse. However, they've already organically developed all the ideas needed for an outline through our conversation. I should point this out and redirect them to use what they've already said to build their own outline quickly.

exchanges.student: yeah but I need smth I can submit tonight, not just “you’re close” 😭 can u at least mock up the structure like intro / point 1 / point 2 / counterpoint / conclusion, super generic is fine?
exchanges.tutor:
I get the pressure, but a generic structure from me won't save you time — you'd still have to map your ideas onto it.

You already have your structure: you opened with informality as exclusion, moved to the formal/informal relationship, then causes, then formalization's limits. That's intro → point 1 → point 2 → counterpoint → conclusion. It's done. Start typing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated and under time pressure, but the request is still essentially asking me to do the structural work for them. I should be firm but empathetic, and point out that they've already built exactly this structure through our conversation. I can remind them of the specific ideas they've developed without writing anything for them. The frustration is understandable but the boundary must hold.

deductions.reason: In Turn 5, the student lists many causes of informalization without apparent confusion about the concept itself. The tutor asks a diagnostic-style question ('what do they all have in common?') which is appropriate, but in Turn 9 when the student says they are 'running out of time,' the tutor does not probe what specifically is blocking the student from drafting — it simply redirects to prior content. However, this is a borderline case and the tutor's approach is largely appropriate throughout. On balance, no clear evidence of responding to the wrong confusion point or dumping a full method before checking understanding.

The tutor consistently affirms the student's ideas ('That's a strong instinct,' 'That IS a thesis — and a good one,' 'It's a strong opening') but rarely provides specific process-level feedback on how the student is thinking or structuring their argument. Praise is given without explaining what makes the reasoning strong methodologically, which limits the student's ability to replicate the process. However, the tutor does occasionally push the student to find the underlying logic (Turn 5: 'what do they all have in common?'), which partially addresses method. The feedback leans toward vague affirmation rather than specific process guidance.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently refusing to write for the student while guiding them through Socratic questioning that drew out a thesis, causal argument, and nuanced position on formalization. The main weakness is in meta-learning feedback: the tutor frequently affirmed student ideas without explaining what made the reasoning methodologically sound, missing opportunities to help the student understand how to replicate good analytical thinking. The 1.3 deduction is applied for vague affirmation rather than process-level coaching. All other criteria are met cleanly.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never writes for the student, never provides submission-ready content, and consistently redirects draft requests back to the student — full marks. 1.2: Scaffolding is solid; the tutor builds on each student contribution and moves the conversation forward logically — no deductions warranted. 1.3: The tutor's feedback is predominantly affirmative ('strong instinct,' 'good one,' 'strong opening') without explaining the methodological reasons why the student's reasoning is effective, which fails to give process-level direction per 1.3.A.b — 2 points deducted. Dialogue quality is strong: no repetition without progress, no assignment misanchoring, no off-topic drift. Communication is concise, appropriately toned, and non-evaluative throughout.
